topic: Solidity basics: contracts, state variables, functions
homework: Write a contract called Piggybank with a state variable 'savings' (uint256) and two functions: deposit() that adds msg.value to savings, and getSavings() that returns it.

topic: Oracles: why contracts can't access external data alone
homework: Sketch (pseudocode is fine) a function that would use a Chainlink price feed to reject a purchase if ETH price is below $1000.
image_prompt: minimalist flat illustration of a bridge connecting an island to the outside world with a data stream, blue purple vector tech art

body:
All pending transactions are visible in the public mempool before being confirmed. This lets bots see a profitable transaction (like a large DEX trade) and insert their own transaction with higher gas to execute first -- called front-running, a form of MEV (Maximal Extractable Value).

Common defenses:
- Commit-reveal schemes (submit a hash first, reveal the actual value later)
- Slippage limits on DEX trades (rejects execution if price moved too much)
- Using private mempools / MEV-protected RPCs (like Flashbots Protect)

WHY IT MATTERS: MEV is a multi-billion-dollar phenomenon on Ethereum. If you're building anything involving trades, auctions, or ordering-sensitive logic, front-running resistance needs to be a design consideration from day one, not an afterthought.

FURTHER READING:
- Flashbots, What is MEV: https://docs.flashbots.net/new-to-mev
- Ethereum.org, MEV: https://ethereum.org/en/developers/docs/mev/
homework: Take the commit-reveal example from this lesson and write the full commit() and reveal() functions with proper require() checks.
image_prompt: minimalist flat illustration of racing figures cutting in line at a queue, blue purple vector tech art

topic: DeFi building blocks: AMMs and liquidity pools
body:
Automated Market Makers (AMMs) like Uniswap let users trade tokens against a pool of liquidity instead of matching buyers/sellers directly. Prices are set algorithmically -- most commonly via the constant product formula: x * y = k.

Core idea: a pool holds reserves of Token A and Token B. When you swap A for B, you add A to the pool and remove B, keeping the product of reserves roughly constant -- which is what causes 'slippage' on large trades.

Simplified swap math:
amountOut = (reserveB * amountIn) / (reserveA + amountIn);

WHY IT MATTERS: AMMs replaced traditional order books as DeFi's dominant trading mechanism because they need no counterparty matching -- just liquidity providers earning fees. Understanding x*y=k is the foundation for understanding most of DeFi trading.

FURTHER READING:
- Uniswap V2 whitepaper: https://uniswap.org/whitepaper.pdf
- Ethereum.org, AMMs: https://ethereum.org/en/developers/docs/smart-contracts/#defi-decentralized-finance
homework: Given reserveA = 1000 and reserveB = 500, calculate by hand how much of token B you'd get for swapping in 100 of token A using x*y=k.
image_prompt: minimalist flat illustration of two connected pools of liquid balancing each other, blue purple vector tech art

topic: Security audits: what auditors actually look for
body:
Audits systematically check contracts against known vulnerability classes: reentrancy, access control gaps, integer issues, oracle manipulation, front-running exposure, unchecked external calls, and logic errors in economic incentives.

A typical process: automated static analysis (tools like Slither) -> manual line-by-line review -> economic/game-theory review -> report with severity-ranked findings -> fixes -> re-review.

EXAMPLE of what gets flagged:
function withdraw(uint256 amt) external {
    (bool ok,) = msg.sender.call{value: amt}(""); // flagged: unchecked external call before state update
    balances[msg.sender] -= amt;
}

WHY IT MATTERS: An audit is not a guarantee of safety -- it's a best-effort review at one point in time. Many exploited protocols WERE audited. Treat audits as risk reduction, not proof of security, and combine them with bug bounties and gradual rollouts (e.g. deposit caps).

FURTHER READING:
- Trail of Bits, Building Secure Contracts: https://github.com/crytic/building-secure-contracts
- Slither static analyzer: https://github.com/crytic/slither
homework: Pick any contract you've written in this series and do a 10-minute self-audit: list 3 things an auditor might flag.
image_prompt: minimalist flat illustration of a magnifying glass inspecting a shield with a checklist, blue purple vector tech art
